author: @robinvince616
text: Your quoted wattage figures seem a bit awry. As you correctly say, 12V at 5A = 60 Watts. But if you run it on 24V, then assuming your ink's resistance remains constant, the power will then increase to 240 Watts (24V x 10A), not 120 Watts.

author: @energycrafts8206
text: Robert, I have looked all over and do not see any comments as to whether your ink/paint is rated food grade?  While your converted toaster oven makes heat, it was not clear if it would be safe for cooking/heating food items for human consumption.  Also the base insulator paint you use may have volatile chemicals released while cooking.  I have a can of BQ grill paint and it does not say if safe for cooking areas.  Most people paint the outside of the grill not the inside nearest the food.  Any advice?

replies.author: @NorthernKitty
replies.text: Despite the title of this video, I felt the intention here was more of a "proof of concept" than an actual "how-to" or "DIY"...  all the points you brought up are things I would believe would be the "next step"... finding all the right materials to make it a safe product.  I see this video more as inspiration for people who wish to pursue it further.

author: @deepforrestalchemy9189
text:
This is a fantastic idea in its infancy, a prototype, there are bound to be some pot holes along the way. It is not my intent to attack or marginalize any individuals opinion in any way. I personally see such great potential here.
@Robert Murray-Smith thank you for your enthusiasm, fascination and wonder which we could all use a bit more of in our lives.

As a couple of people have already suggested increasing the thickness of the ink coats would decrease the resistance I=V/R or create more panels in parallel and so increase the wattage, maybe bring the oven up to 300W if possible so it can be run to be more thermally stable when the door is opened briefly or a colder mass is introduced.

I see that 300W 12V ceramic heating cartridges are easily and economically obtained from ebay, ali express and bangood. Embed one of these in a Graphite or aluminium block maximised for high surface area and utilise this element as an rapid boost assist at the bottom of the oven. 


Switching between the two heating sources may give you a better thermal profile for cooking which can easily be implemented using PID algorithms solid state relays and an arduino. That would be my approach to developing the idea into a working off grid culinary device.


With a little research you could strive to mimic the thermal profiles of the worlds best ovens - that would make the final product highly sought world-wide and therein the marketing tool with which to promote it.

Thank-you 
@goldmagnet for the inspiration and motivation to make this comment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Mate - thank you for taking the time to do that because you are right this is a product in it's infancy. I am thinking of going off grid myself but I live in a typical UK suburb Andy thought is to make one change at a time until I am all 12V - lights are easy but I was looking at cooking and couldn't find anything - so me being me - I decided to make it and I thought it was useful - but also something to follow up with I think - so the advice is well well appreciated

replies.author: @deepforrestalchemy9189
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith absolutely my pleasure and I am always happy to help if I am able.

I am uncertain of weather these ideas will come to anything as I was shooting from the hip; but  it has always been my experience that positive momentum is generated by the open workshopping of ideas and initial Impressions.

I applaud your work mate and as always look forward to your next foray into the frontiers of science. 
You inspire in me an appreciation of how paradigms (ideas) are manifested as a reality through articulation collaboration consultation iteration and feedback. Moving through working theory to a reality. The thing is to be confident and courageous enough to put your idea out there in the first place and follow it through.
More often than not if not born of necessity they are from the realm of fiction such as the communicators and portable library computers of Star Trek becoming mobile phones and laptops.
Kudos on being an innovator.

author: @energycrafts8206
text:
Nice upgrade Robert.  Nice results.  I see lots of applications.  Soon you will be rich and can go to the tropics and sip hard to pronounce umbrella drinks on the beach as often as desired and get out of the lab for a bit!  

But as an electronics tech over 20 years, I have to inform you that resistance is the most efficient means of electric to heat conversion.  So the original oven heater elements are like 99% efficient all ready and likely the same for your ink heat conversion per watt input.  So no real heat gain/watt in your case and little room to improve it.  Also, your DC low watt power can not compete with the original KW power rated elements in terms of heating a load (food etc.).  

I agree you  need temperature in the box to be the same as original design, but maybe you forget that there is the issue of heat capacity.  When you turn on the oven and it reaches the desired temp., the heat only had to heat up the air within:  little work, took little time and  little power.  But if you throw in there a large chicken, now the heat capacity demand is very much increased and it will take an incredibly longer TIME to heat the bird to that same temp as for just the air.  It makes little difference that extra insulation was installed.  The fact is the chicken represents a lot more atoms/molecules to heat up as compared to air molecules alone.

I encounter the same heat capacity vs KW issue in solar cooking.  A crock pot of vegetables and beef as a stew can cook and be ready to eat in just an hour and half or so, but with a parabolic reflector cooker it can take like 4 or 5 hours to do the same.  Even though the crock and the solar reflector can reach the same temps, they are not the same kWh rated, the reflector being lower powered.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I absolutely agree mate - but the original heaters ( and heaters in other ovens) are often bars spaced in the heating cavity - the paint allows intimate contact with the body of the cooker so transfer is likely to be improved over a greater surface area - giving a more even temperature profile but there is no improvement in efficiency of the heater - like you say likely to be 99% as most resistive heaters are but I suspect there is an improvement in the efficiency of heat transfer - I guess the same thing could well be accomplished by using a fan - which is done. The original oven had no insulation - A situation I have seen many times as I have taken quite a few of these apart and as heat application has two main phases - here I mean ramp and soak - we aren't looking at improving the energy efficiency of the ramp phase as that is fixed based on thermal load - but rather the soak phase which is a balance between inputs and losses. Basically if you put a big chicken in there to get it to temp will be the same energy cost - but to cook it for the 35 mins a kilo required should well be cheaper.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: front, back and bottom mate - I put it on three sides

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: Total logic and well said.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So how long would it take to cook a chicken safely in the 120 watt oven Rob, I would love to know ?

replies.author: @energycrafts8206
replies.text:
Robert, Hi.  Thanks for replying so courteously.  Yep I agree about the insulation.  I have a similar oven and there is no insulation anywhere!  Not even at the pcb!  Insulation would reduce heat loss, hence less $ spent on electric to reach desired load temp.  

It would be great if you took 2 ovens, one normal and your version side by side and cooked at a  useful heat load -- such as chickens and note time and temp to completion.  I know you like bread, but it is not much of a challenge as even low grade solar cooking will make bread -- with enough sun time, thou slow.  Back in the 70's I remember my sister getting a toy cooking oven that heated simple items by just a 100W light bulb.  I think it could, beyond heat up of sandwiches, also COOK cupcakes.  However, the muffin tins were very small.  If I recall correctly, the cupcake ingredients came prepackaged (manuf. secret) and no doubt the recipe was optimized for low temp cooking. 

Finally, a different subject.  For solar energy cooking, having a pot (or solar water piping collector) blackened as much as possible greatly improves heat production at the pot as you know.  As there are different grades of black, do you know if your ink is any blacker (IR wise) than my regular use of charcoal painting or use of engine rated flat black paint?  Now there is an instant needed product of high demand if you can compete in the solar market.  Yes there are some special solar coatings out there but they are crazy expensive.

author: @oddjobbobb
text: Maybe I missed something in the disassembly... Isn’t there a layer of insulation between the very inside of the box and the metal sheeting to which you applied the stove paint and your conductive ink? Most stoves (albeit, they are home stoves but same principle) that I have worked on have a insulation around the inside box for two reasons, keep the heat in the cooking are and to keep heat away from the electrical bits and from people standing around in the kitchen and the cabinet into/onto which the oven is placed.

replies.author: @oddjobbobb
replies.text: Sorry, I see it now. The insulation was replaced onto the painted surface. Thank you. Very informative.

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yes you're right. Do not forget about thermal capacity / density. To heat a 100ml of water on classic 2kW hot plate, the water will boil in less that 2 min. A hot plate with 240W on same temperature will need around 20 min or more.

text: If we use 120 W instead of 3000 W,it takes more time to cook and possibly spoils baking cakes!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not really mate - it's not about watts it's about temperature - the main reason normal ovens use such a lot of energy to get to temperature is that they are poorly insulated and have inefficient heaters - that's all really - this oven is efficient at heat transfer and i put a lot of insulation in that's why the power consumption is low but temperature is the same - no magic mate - just common sense really

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Robert your reply is terrible from a science perspective, so inaccurate. The analogy is you are basically saying a battery is all about volts ! You put a cup of water in your oven, and compare it to a 3kw oven, how long would they both take to boil, yours with 120 watts input compared to a 3kw unit. If you are honest you know that it would take much longer to cook anything with any mass in this oven, compared to a normal oven, thats all the poster was saying.   We get that the ink is good, but you are selling it like snake oil.

replies.author: @laszlon.4424
replies.text: I'm no kitchen guru, but you'd simply preheat the oven until the desired temperature is reached & cook as per usual.  Every oven has it's own quirks that you learn to adjust for.

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @laszlon.4424 You are Rob are not taking into account thermal mass, you cant melt an iceberg with red hot coin, just because the insulated oven gets hot does not mean it can cook anything.. A candle flame is as hot as your oven, but it would take forever to cook a meal with a candle, you need the power (watts).

replies.author: @dyukk8844
replies.text: @goldmagnet9013 Yes I totally agree with @goldmagnet on thermal mass and the power. I think cyrex said in one of his videos that the power consumption on classic hot plate heaters and painted carbon ink hot plate heaters are almost the same, if you want to heat something on same time. Only the design of the heater element is different. Are not electric heat elements 99% efficient?

author: @batterymooch
text:
Brilliant! Thank you for this video.
I’ve been considering building a thermal chamber for cell testing but never really found an elegant solution for a small size box (I only need to fit a pair of 21700 size cells or smaller in the box). Using your ink gives me all kinds of freedom when choosing the box size and shape. All I need to do is use a small fan for circulating the air (and a temp controller, of course) and it should work great. Just fantastic!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it should be awesome mate - would you do a vid - i'd love to see

replies.author: @batterymooch
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith yes, definitely. Many of my subscribers are tech-minded and would be interested too. I’m  actually wondering if I use a small enough chamber, matte black painted interior and about 700cm3 in my design, if I can get away with not using a fan. Definitely a lot of testing coming up!

author: @Buzzhumma
text:
Hi Robert
 I made a oven pie warmer out of plywood and a 15 0hm resistor 10 watt wrapped in 1 mm aluminium as a heat sink. I use it in my campervan .It was initially just for proof of concept but i found it works perfect but after watching this I will use a small microwave oven and paint a resistor as you have 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - do vid to - love to see - cheers

author: @Killianwsh
text: Awesome! My sister was just grumbling about the cost of replacing the galley bits in her boat! I predict a diy in her future! :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate -do a vid and if you have questions during the build - drop me a line

replies.author: @Killianwsh
replies.text: Will do! Thanks! I'm  pretty sure she's gonna love this, as will skads of her sailing pals. Galley kit, below decks or otherwise, is monstrously expensive as conventional electrics are grossly power hungry on the batteries and propane is even more so as, propane being heavier than air , if it leaks can fill the whole boat in no time. Then...one spark..even from St. Elmos fire. ....BOOM B.D.O.B. (bad day on boat) :-)
